comment: Both defined by the same temporal-persistence criterion (wholly present at each time of existence). Not `exact-equivalence-candidate` because the two sources' philosophical framing differs (realist vs. cognitive/linguistic per `note-002`), so full interchangeability across every context is not asserted.
Same rationale as mapping-001, inverse pairing.
UFO continues DOLCE's endurant/perdurant lineage using the same temporal-persistence criterion.
Same rationale as mapping-003, inverse pairing.

**Corrected (2026-07-06, full-corpus audit)** from `exact-equivalence-candidate` (itself raised 2026-07-01 from `medium`): both primary sources are directly verified and their endurant definitions are near-verbatim ("wholly present... at any time they are present" vs. "individuals that exist in time with all their parts") — UFO's endurant/perdurant split directly continues DOLCE's, confirmed rather than merely asserted. But `note-002` documents that DOLCE's split is motivated by an explicit cognitive-bias disclaimer ("we do not commit to the fact that both these kinds of entity really exist"), while UFO's is grounded in a four-category ontology merged from Bunge-Wand-Weber conceptual-modeling work — a different philosophical lineage, the same kind of divergence `mapping-001`/`mapping-003` already use to withhold `exact-equivalence-candidate` for BFO-DOLCE and BFO-UFO. Applying that same standard consistently: `close-match`, not `exact-equivalence-candidate`, for this pair too. `high` confidence in `close-match`: both the near-verbatim wording and the philosophical divergence are directly evidenced, not open questions.

Structural analog only: SUMO's Object sits under a physicality partition, not a temporal-persistence one. Whether SUMO:Object behaves like a continuant/endurant in practice is not confirmed by Niles & Pease (2001) alone.

Both defined by a temporal-presence criterion (BFO: wholly present at each time it exists; GFO: "exists wholly at exactly one time boundary"), but GFO's Presential is defined over discrete time-boundaries specifically, a narrower formal commitment than BFO's continuant. Medium rather than high pending a closer read of GFO's time-boundary/chronoid theory.

Both sources use the identical term "occurrent" with matching temporal-extension criteria (BFO: "unfolds itself in time"; GFO: "have temporal parts... happen in time"). Highest-confidence mapping among the four new sources' correspondences, on par with the BFO/DOLCE/UFO occurrent mappings.

YAMATO uses "Continuant" as its own top-level label with an explicitly stated 3D/persistence orientation, the closest terminological and conceptual match to BFO of any `ADR-0015` source. Not `exact-equivalence-candidate` because YAMATO explicitly treats continuant and occurrent as mutually dependent rather than either being ontologically prior — a stated philosophical difference from BFO's framing.

Same rationale as mapping-010, inverse pairing. YAMATO additionally distinguishes process (can change) from event (cannot) within occurrent, a finer-grained split than BFO makes explicitly at this level — noted, not resolved, per `uncertainty-003`.

Structural analog only, same rationale as SUMO's Object mapping (mapping-006): TUpper's `object` is defined negatively (by exclusion of timepoint/activity/activity-occurrence) rather than by a positive temporal-persistence criterion, so equivalence to BFO's continuant is not established by the definition text alone.

Same rationale as mapping-012. `activity_occurrence` is tied to a specific `activity` via `occurrence_of`, a PSL-derived structure not obviously equivalent to BFO's broader occurrent (which also covers temporal/spatiotemporal regions).

Structural analog only, same rationale as SUMO's Object mapping (mapping-006): GUM's SimpleThing/Process split is linguistically motivated (participant vs. verb-realized "goings-on"), not a temporal-persistence criterion.
